description: File conforms to the FITS standard
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; K2; GALEX; Spitzer; Chandra; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates whether the FITS file strictly adheres to the basic FITS standard. A value of 'T' (True) means the file structure is standard, making it compatible with FITS-compliant software. It's a foundational check to ensure proper file parsing.
relevance_comment: This keyword is purely structural, ensuring file readability by software, with no direct information value for end-users.

description: Number of bits per data pixel. Allowed values: 8, 16, 32, 64 (integer), -32 (float), -64 (double).
hdu: PRIMARY; SCI; ERR; DQ; GROUPDQ; REFOUT
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; K2; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the number of bits used to represent each individual pixel's data value (e.g., 16-bit integer, 32-bit floating-point). It defines the fundamental data type and precision of the raw pixel values within the image or data array. Understanding BITPIX can help users interpret the range and fidelity of the stored data.
relevance_comment: While not directly interacted with, knowing the underlying data type can be useful for advanced users interpreting data precision or performing specific analyses.

description: Number of data array dimensions
hdu: PRIMARY; SCI; ERR; DQ; GROUPDQ; REFOUT
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the number of dimensions in the data array, such as a 2D image (NAXIS=2) or a 3D data cube (NAXIS=3). It tells the user if they are looking at a simple image, a time series, or a more complex dataset like a spectral cube. This directly impacts how the data is visualized and explored.
relevance_comment: This keyword is important as it defines the fundamental dimensionality of the dataset, directly affecting how an app would display and allow interaction with the data.

description: Length of data axis 1 (columns)
hdu: PRIMARY; SCI; ERR; DQ; GROUPDQ; REFOUT
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the size of the first dimension of the data array, which typically represents the number of columns in an image. For a 2D image, this tells you the width of the image in pixels. It's essential for understanding the spatial extent of the data.
relevance_comment: Users frequently need to know the width of an image, making this keyword highly relevant for display and spatial context.

description: Length of data axis 2 (rows)
hdu: PRIMARY; SCI; ERR; DQ; GROUPDQ; REFOUT
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the size of the second dimension of the data array, which typically represents the number of rows in an image. For a 2D image, this tells you the height of the image in pixels. It's essential for understanding the spatial extent of the data.
relevance_comment: Users frequently need to know the height of an image, making this keyword highly relevant for display and spatial context.

description: Length of data axis 3 (planes/channels)
content: This keyword specifies the length or size of the third dimension of the data array. For astronomical data, this often represents distinct elements like different spectral channels, multiple filters, or a time series of images, indicating that the data is a 3D cube rather than a simple 2D image.
relevance_comment: It is commonly useful for users to understand the dimensionality of data cubes, such as the number of spectral channels or filter planes, for analysis and visualization.

description: Length of data axis 4
content: This keyword indicates the size of the fourth dimension of the data array. While less common than 2D or 3D data, a fourth axis can represent additional data aspects like polarization states, dither positions, or multiple data products within a single file.
relevance_comment: While less common than 3D data, knowing the extent of a fourth axis is useful for advanced users working with complex, higher-dimensional datasets.

description: File may contain standard extensions
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX
content: This boolean keyword, found in the primary header, indicates whether the FITS file might contain standard extensions beyond the primary data array. These extensions are separate HDUs (Header Data Units) that can hold additional data like error maps, data quality flags, or tabular information.
relevance_comment: This structural keyword informs software about the potential for extensions, but users are more interested in the content of specific extensions rather than this general flag.

description: Number of file extensions
content: This keyword specifies the total number of standard FITS extensions present in the file. It provides a count of how many additional data blocks (which can be images, tables, etc.) are included alongside the primary data.
relevance_comment: While providing a count of extensions, this keyword is primarily for software parsing, and users are typically more interested in the specific types and content of individual extensions.

description: Number of bytes of supplemental data (parameter count)
comment: number of parameter bytes following data table
content: This keyword is used in FITS binary tables to specify the number of bytes used for supplemental data, particularly for variable-length array (VLA) columns. It points to the location where the actual variable-length data elements are stored within the data block.
relevance_comment: This keyword is purely internal and structural, used by FITS readers to correctly parse variable-length array data within tables, with no direct relevance for end-users.

description: Number of data groups
content: GCOUNT specifies the number of data groups present in a variable-length array FITS file. It is primarily used for specialized FITS structures that contain multiple, often differing, data arrays within a single HDU, rather than a simple image or table.
relevance_comment: This keyword is a low-level structural detail for specialized FITS files and holds almost no direct value for typical end-users.

description: Type of extension (IMAGE, BINTABLE, TABLE)
hdu: SCI; ERR; DQ; GROUPDQ; REFOUT; ASDF
content: XTENSION indicates the type of data stored in a FITS extension, such as an IMAGE (for images or multi-dimensional arrays), BINTABLE (for binary tables), or TABLE (for ASCII tables). This keyword helps software understand how to correctly interpret the data structure within that specific FITS High Definition Unit (HDU).
relevance_comment: While structural, XTENSION informs the user about the fundamental type of data (image vs. table) in an HDU, which impacts how they might interact with it.

description: Name of the extension
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX
content: EXTNAME provides a descriptive name for a FITS extension, helping to identify its content and purpose within a multi-HDU FITS file. For example, it might be 'SCI' for science data, 'ERR' for error data, or 'DQ' for data quality information.
relevance_comment: EXTNAME is highly useful for users to quickly identify the purpose and content of different data components within a multi-HDU FITS file.

description: Version number of the extension
content: EXTVER specifies a version number for a FITS extension, which is useful when multiple extensions within the same FITS file share the same EXTNAME. This allows for distinguishing between different versions or instances of a particular data type, like 'SCI, EXTVER=1' and 'SCI, EXTVER=2'.
relevance_comment: EXTVER is useful for distinguishing between multiple instances or versions of similarly named data extensions, particularly in complex multi-HDU files.

description: Hierarchical level of the extension
content: EXTLEVEL indicates the hierarchical level of a FITS extension, primarily used in more complex FITS structures where data might be nested or organized in a tree-like fashion. It helps define the relationship between extensions within a multi-HDU FITS file.
relevance_comment: EXTLEVEL is a highly technical structural keyword for complex FITS hierarchies and has minimal direct relevance for typical end-users.

description: Linear scaling factor for data values: physical = BZERO + BSCALE × array_value
comment: scale factor for array value to physical value
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the linear scaling factor used to convert raw, stored pixel values into meaningful physical units. The actual physical value of a pixel is calculated as (BZERO + BSCALE × array_value). It is crucial for converting arbitrary digital numbers into scientifically interpretable quantities like flux or intensity.
relevance_comment: It's essential for converting raw pixel counts into meaningful physical units, which is key for quantitative analysis and accurate data display.

description: Zero point offset for data values: physical = BZERO + BSCALE × array_value
comment: physical value for an array value of zero
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword represents the zero-point offset applied when converting raw, stored pixel values into physical units. The formula for conversion is (BZERO + BSCALE × array_value), meaning BZERO shifts the entire scale. It works in conjunction with BSCALE to ensure pixel values reflect true physical quantities.
relevance_comment: It works with BSCALE to convert raw pixel values into meaningful physical units, critical for quantitative analysis and accurate data representation.

description: Physical unit of the data array values (e.g., DN, MJy/sr, electrons/s)
comment: physical units of the data array values
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; Spitzer
content: This keyword specifies the physical units of the data array values after they have been scaled by BSCALE and BZERO. Examples include 'Jy/pixel' for flux density, 'MJy/sr' for surface brightness, or 'electrons/s' for count rates. It is absolutely fundamental for understanding what the numbers in the image or table actually represent.
relevance_comment: This keyword directly tells users the physical units of the data, which is fundamental for understanding and interpreting any quantitative measurement.

description: Type of data (e.g., surface brightness, flux)
content: This keyword provides a high-level description of the type of data contained within the array, such as 'FLUX', 'SURFACE BRIGHTNESS', 'ERROR', or 'DQ' (Data Quality). It helps users quickly understand the nature and purpose of the data, offering context beyond just the units.
relevance_comment: It helps users understand the specific nature or type of data contained in the array, providing crucial context for interpretation.

description: Value representing undefined pixels in integer arrays
content: For FITS files storing integer data, this keyword specifies a particular integer value that should be interpreted as 'no data,' 'undefined,' or 'missing.' Pixels with this value are typically ignored in calculations or rendered distinctly in visualizations, preventing erroneous analysis of invalid regions.
relevance_comment: It identifies undefined or invalid pixel values, which is important for accurate data visualization and preventing incorrect analysis of missing information.

description: Maximum data value in the array
content: This keyword stores the maximum pixel value found within the image data array. It's important for understanding the range of brightness in an image, helping users set appropriate display scales to visualize features, and quickly identify potentially saturated regions.
relevance_comment: This value is commonly useful for setting display ranges and quickly assessing data quality or dynamic range.

description: Minimum data value in the array
content: This keyword stores the minimum pixel value found within the image data array. It helps users understand the lowest brightness levels in an image, useful for setting display scales, identifying background levels, or assessing noise characteristics.
relevance_comment: This value is commonly useful for setting display ranges and quickly assessing data quality or background levels.

description: File creation date in UTC (YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss format)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the UTC date and time when the FITS file itself was created. It's a file system timestamp rather than an observation time, useful for tracking data provenance and understanding when a particular data product was generated or last processed.
relevance_comment: This keyword is moderately useful for data management and provenance tracking, but not directly tied to the astronomical observation itself.

description: Date of observation start (UTC) in YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss format
comment: UTC date at start of exposure
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; K2; GALEX; Spitzer; Chandra; Pan-STARRS; SwiftUVOT; XMM-OM
content: This keyword records the UTC date and time when the astronomical observation actually began. It is a fundamental piece of information for any astronomical data, allowing users to know exactly when the celestial event was captured and enabling time-domain studies.
relevance_comment: This is an essential piece of metadata for understanding when the astronomical observation occurred.

description: Date-time of observation start (ISO 8601 format)
content: Similar to DATE-OBS, this keyword specifies the UTC date and time of the observation's beginning, often in a standardized ISO 8601 format. It is crucial for precise timing analyses, correlating observations with other datasets, and placing the observation in its temporal context.
relevance_comment: This is an essential piece of metadata for understanding when the astronomical observation occurred, especially with its precise time component.

description: Date-time of observation end (ISO 8601 format)
content: This keyword specifies the exact date and time when the observation concluded, formatted according to the ISO 8601 standard. It's crucial for understanding the total duration of an exposure and for precise scheduling or time-series analysis when combined with the start time.
relevance_comment: It is very useful for precise timing, calculating exposure duration, and understanding the full observational window for analysis.

description: Time of observation start (UTC) in hh:mm:ss format
comment: UTC time at start of exposure
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the precise Universal Coordinated Time (UTC) when the astronomical observation officially began, typically in a hh:mm:ss format. It's fundamental for time-stamping data, correlating observations with events, and determining sidereal time at the start of the exposure.
relevance_comment: This keyword is essential for all users to understand the precise start time of the observation.

description: Principal time system for time-related keywords (e.g., UTC, TDB, TT)
comment: principal time system for time-related keywords
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler
content: This keyword specifies the particular time scale system used for other time-related keywords in the FITS header, such as UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) or TDB (Barycentric Dynamical Time). It's vital for ensuring accurate conversions and comparisons of timing information across different datasets or astronomical calculations.
relevance_comment: This keyword is important for advanced users who need to perform precise time conversions or compare datasets with different time systems.

description: Default unit for time values (e.g., 's' for seconds, 'd' for days)
comment: Default unit applicable to all time values
content: This keyword defines the standard unit of measure for any numerical time values present in the FITS header that don't explicitly specify their own units, such as 's' for seconds or 'd' for days. It ensures unambiguous interpretation of time-related data.
relevance_comment: This keyword is important for correctly interpreting numerical time values and ensuring proper display or calculation of durations.

description: Modified Julian Date of observation start
comment: Modified Julian Date of observation start
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword provides the Modified Julian Date (MJD) corresponding to the start of the observation, expressed as a floating-point number. MJD is a widely used astronomical time system that simplifies calculations by counting days continuously from a specific epoch, making it ideal for precise timing and long-term event tracking.
relevance_comment: This keyword is highly relevant for precise astronomical timing, calculations, and comparing observation epochs across different datasets.

description: Modified Julian Date of exposure start
content: This keyword specifies the exact Modified Julian Date (MJD) when an astronomical observation began. It's crucial for precisely timing events, correlating observations with other data, and accounting for celestial motion or instrumental changes over time.
relevance_comment: Users often need the precise start time of an observation for analysis, correlation, and historical context.

description: Modified Julian Date of exposure midpoint
comment: Modified Julian Date of exposure midpoint
content: This keyword gives the Modified Julian Date (MJD) corresponding to the exact midpoint of an astronomical exposure. It's often considered the most representative time for the observation, particularly for objects with changing positions or fluxes.
relevance_comment: The midpoint time is frequently the most representative time for an observation, essential for many astronomical analyses.

description: Modified Julian Date of exposure midpoint (average)
content: Similar to MJD-MID, this keyword represents the average or midpoint Modified Julian Date (MJD) of an exposure or data collection period. It provides a single representative timestamp for the observation, important for linking data to ephemerides or other time-variable phenomena.
relevance_comment: This keyword provides a key representative timestamp for the observation, crucial for time-sensitive analysis.

description: Modified Julian Date of exposure end
content: This keyword indicates the Modified Julian Date (MJD) at which an astronomical observation concluded. It's important for calculating the total duration of an exposure and for understanding the temporal boundaries of the collected data.
relevance_comment: The end time of an observation is vital for understanding the observation's duration and its temporal context.

description: Exposure start time in Modified Julian Date
content: This keyword provides the start time of the exposure in Modified Julian Date (MJD). It's a fundamental timestamp indicating when data collection began, essential for understanding the temporal context of an observation and for precise astrometric or photometric analysis.
relevance_comment: Users need the precise start time of an observation to understand its temporal context and for accurate data analysis.

description: Exposure mid time in Modified Julian Date
content: This keyword indicates the exact midpoint of the observation's exposure, expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). It's crucial for time-sensitive analyses, such as tracking variable stars, analyzing transient events, or aligning observations with precise ephemeris data. Knowing the precise middle of the exposure provides the most accurate timestamp for the collected photons.
relevance_comment: Provides a precise timestamp for the observation, critical for time-domain astronomy or linking to ephemeris data.

description: Exposure end time in Modified Julian Date
content: This keyword specifies the exact moment the observation concluded, expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). While EXPMID gives the center point, EXPEND is vital for understanding the full duration of an event, coordinating follow-up observations, or precisely marking the end of data collection in time-sensitive studies. It complements EXPMID and EXPTIME to fully define the observation's temporal bounds.
relevance_comment: Pinpoints the conclusion of the observation, useful for precise event timing and coordinating subsequent data.

description: Exposure time in seconds
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; K2; GALEX; Spitzer; Chandra; Pan-STARRS; SwiftUVOT; XMM-OM
content: This is a fundamental keyword indicating the total duration, in seconds, that the detector was open and collecting light from the astronomical source. It directly impacts the signal-to-noise ratio and the overall brightness of the captured image, making it essential for understanding the quality and depth of the observation. A longer exposure generally means more light collected and a clearer image.
relevance_comment: A fundamental parameter that directly indicates how long light was collected, crucial for understanding signal strength and data quality.

description: Effective exposure time corrected for dead time (seconds)
content: This keyword represents the 'effective' exposure time, corrected for any periods where the detector was not actively collecting useful photons, known as 'dead time.' This can include time spent on readout, shutter operations, or other instrument overheads. It provides a more accurate measure of the true integration duration for scientific analysis, particularly for precise photometry and flux calculations.
relevance_comment: Provides a more accurate exposure duration by accounting for periods of detector inactivity, important for precise scientific measurements.

description: Effective integration time (seconds)
content: This keyword specifies the effective integration time, which is the total time the detector was actively accumulating photons, often accounting for instrument-specific readout patterns or multiple non-destructive reads within an exposure. It's particularly relevant for instruments with complex observing strategies, like JWST, to provide a precise measure of the actual photon collection duration, which can differ from the raw exposure time.
relevance_comment: A specialized measure of actual photon collection time, particularly useful for understanding complex instrument readout modes like those on JWST.

description: Total duration of exposure: integration time × number of integrations (seconds)
content: This keyword indicates the total time the detector was actively collecting light, calculated as the product of the integration time for a single readout and the total number of such integrations. It represents the overall light-gathering duration for the observation, ignoring overheads. This is a fundamental measure of the exposure's depth.
relevance_comment: Users frequently check this value to understand how much light was collected for the observation and its overall depth.

description: Effective exposure time in seconds (in SCI extension)
content: This keyword provides the effective exposure time in seconds, typically after accounting for any periods where the detector was not actively collecting usable photons (e.g., due to detector resets or non-science frames). It's a refined and often more accurate measure of the actual science exposure duration.
relevance_comment: This value provides the most accurate measure of the effective integration time for the science data, which is crucial for analysis.

description: Total elapsed exposure time in seconds
content: This keyword records the total wall-clock time that elapsed during the entire exposure, from the start of the first integration to the end of the last. It includes not only the photon collection time but also any overheads between integrations or readouts, providing insight into the total observation duration.
relevance_comment: While useful for understanding observation overheads and scheduling, it's less critical for interpreting the direct science content of an individual image compared to effective exposure time.

description: Total exposure time of all pointings in a resampled product (seconds)
comment: [s] Total exposure time for all pointings
content: Specific to resampled or combined data products, this keyword indicates the sum of the effective exposure times from all individual input observations that contributed to the final product. It represents the total light-gathering duration accumulated in the combined image, crucial for assessing its signal-to-noise.
relevance_comment: For resampled products, this value is essential for understanding the overall depth and signal-to-noise of the combined image.

description: Number of integrations within the exposure
content: This keyword specifies the number of individual integrations performed within a single exposure. An integration is a sequence of detector reads, and knowing this value helps understand how the total exposure time was built up and can be important for noise characterization or advanced data processing.
relevance_comment: This parameter is valuable for advanced users interested in data quality, noise properties, and understanding the observation strategy.

description: Number of groups per integration
content: This keyword indicates the number of 'groups' of detector reads collected during a single integration for JWST. A group is an average of several individual frames, and more groups generally lead to longer effective exposure times and improved signal-to-noise ratio in the final data.
relevance_comment: This keyword is crucial for understanding the exposure strategy and effective integration time in non-destructive read modes, directly impacting data quality and analysis.

description: Number of frames in a group
content: This keyword specifies the number of individual 'frames' (single detector reads) that are averaged together to form a single 'group' within a JWST exposure. Increasing the number of frames per group can help reduce read noise but might also influence cosmic ray persistence.
relevance_comment: This keyword defines the number of individual detector reads averaged into each group, directly influencing read noise characteristics and the data acquisition strategy.

description: Number of frames skipped between groups
comment: Number of frames dropped between groups
content: This keyword indicates the number of individual frames that are skipped or discarded between consecutive 'groups' during a JWST exposure. These skipped frames do not contribute to the final averaged signal but are part of the detector readout sequence.
relevance_comment: This keyword describes skipped frames between groups, affecting the total observation timeline but not directly contributing to the integrated signal, making it more relevant for advanced timing analysis.

description: Number of A/D samples per pixel read
comment: Number of A/D samples per pixel
content: This keyword denotes the number of analog-to-digital (A/D) samples taken for each pixel read within a single frame. Multiple samples can be averaged to reduce the A/D conversion noise.
relevance_comment: This keyword details the number of A/D samples per pixel read, a low-level detector characteristic primarily relevant for detailed noise analysis or instrument performance studies.

description: Time between samples (microseconds)
content: This keyword provides the time interval, in microseconds, between consecutive analog-to-digital samples taken for a given pixel. It describes the temporal resolution of the detector's sampling process.
relevance_comment: This keyword indicates the time between A/D samples, a very low-level timing parameter critical for instrument engineers but generally not for typical end-user data interpretation.

description: Time between start of successive frames (seconds)
content: This keyword specifies the time duration, in seconds, between the start of one data frame and the start of the next consecutive frame within a multi-frame exposure. It's crucial for understanding the timing of short integrations or how quickly data is read out in certain low-level modes, especially relevant for time-series observations.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding low-level acquisition details and time-series analysis, but less so for general high-level science users.

description: Time between start of successive groups (seconds)
content: This keyword indicates the time, in seconds, from the start of one data group to the start of the next group in a detector ramp. In JWST's non-destructive readout, a "group" represents a set of frames combined. This value is fundamental to understanding the sampling rate of the detector ramp and calculating the total integration time per exposure.
relevance_comment: Crucial for understanding the integration time, noise properties, and overall data acquisition structure of JWST ramp data.

description: Readout pattern name (pre-defined NFRAMES, GROUPGAP, NRESETS combination)
content: This keyword specifies the pre-defined name of the detector readout pattern used for the observation (e.g., RAPID, BRIGHT1, MEDIUM8). This pattern dictates parameters like the number of frames per group, gaps between groups, and reset frequency, which directly affect the data sampling strategy, noise characteristics, and dynamic range.
relevance_comment: Essential for understanding the data acquisition mode, noise properties, and potential saturation limits of JWST observations.

description: Exposure type (e.g., MIR_IMAGE, NRC_IMAGE, NRS_MSASPEC)
comment: Type of data in the exposure
content: This keyword identifies the broad category of the observation, indicating the instrument mode and type of data acquired (e.g., direct imaging, spectroscopy, or coronagraphy). It also specifies which instrument (e.g., MIRI, NIRCam, NIRSpec) was used, making it fundamental for understanding the nature of the observation.
relevance_comment: Provides immediate context about the instrument and scientific purpose of the data, essential for all users.

description: Whether exposure is PRIME, PARALLEL_COORDINATED, or PARALLEL_PURE
content: This keyword indicates whether the observation was the primary (PRIME) observation driving the telescope pointing, a coordinated parallel observation with another instrument, or a pure parallel observation taken opportunistically without dictating pointing. This affects how the observation fits into the overall observing program and its context within other datasets.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the observation's role in the overall observing program and assessing its primary science intent and context.

description: Monotonically increasing exposure count within a visit
comment: Running count of exposures in visit
content: This number indicates the sequence of the current exposure within a larger set of observations taken during a single observing program or 'visit'. It helps users understand if this particular data file represents the first, second, or a subsequent exposure in a series.
relevance_comment: This keyword is useful for advanced users to understand the sequence of observations within a visit or program, aiding in specific analysis workflows.

description: Post-processing method used in FPAP; maps to readout pattern
content: This keyword describes the specific readout pattern or post-processing method used by the instrument's detector to acquire the raw data. It influences how the raw data was collected and processed, which can affect its characteristics and quality.
relevance_comment: This is a technical detail about instrument readout that might interest advanced users for specific analysis or understanding data artifacts.

description: Science telemetry indicated data problems (T or F)
content: This boolean flag indicates whether the instrument's internal telemetry detected any significant problems during the acquisition of this specific science data. A 'True' value suggests that there may have been issues with the data collection for this exposure.
relevance_comment: This keyword is highly relevant as it directly indicates potential quality issues with the science data, which is crucial for any user evaluating its reliability.

description: Number of frames dropped prior to first integration
comment: Number of frames dropped before 1st integration
content: This keyword indicates the number of individual detector frames that were discarded or 'dropped' by the instrument prior to the very first scientific integration (exposure) beginning. This process is typically done to allow the detector to stabilize before collecting science data.
relevance_comment: This is a very specific, low-level instrument operational detail about detector stabilization, rarely relevant for general or most advanced user analysis.

description: Number of frames dropped between integrations
comment: Number of frames dropped between integrations
content: This keyword reports the number of detector frames dropped by the instrument between consecutive scientific integrations within a longer exposure sequence. Similar to DRPFRMS1, it relates to the internal process of detector stabilization during an observation.
relevance_comment: This is a very specific, low-level instrument operational detail about detector stabilization, rarely relevant for general or most advanced user analysis.

description: Number of resets separating integrations within an exposure
comment: Number of reset frames between integrations
content: This keyword indicates the number of times the detector pixels were reset between individual data collection periods, known as integrations, within a single observation. It's an internal operational parameter that affects how the detector manages charge and can influence noise properties. While important for instrument experts, it rarely impacts typical data interpretation.
relevance_comment: This is a low-level detector operational detail mostly relevant for instrument specialists or deep noise analysis.

description: Number of resets at the start of exposure
comment: Number of reset frames at start of exposure
content: This keyword specifies how many times the detector pixels were reset immediately before the first data integration of an exposure began. It's a fundamental parameter for initializing the detector and managing its dark current and noise characteristics at the start of an observation. Like NRESETS, it's primarily of interest to instrument engineers.
relevance_comment: This is a low-level detector operational detail mostly relevant for instrument specialists or deep noise analysis.

description: Number of detector amplifier outputs used (1, 4, or 5)
content: This keyword indicates the number of electronic amplifier channels used to read out the detector's data. Using multiple outputs (e.g., 1, 4, or 5 for JWST) typically allows for faster data acquisition but can introduce specific readout noise patterns or variations across the detector. Understanding this can be important for identifying certain data artifacts or optimizing data processing.
relevance_comment: This instrument configuration detail affects data quality and potential artifacts, useful for advanced users or troubleshooting.

description: Sensor Chip Assembly number (1–18)
content: This keyword identifies the specific Sensor Chip Assembly (SCA), a physical detector chip, that was used to acquire the data. Many JWST instruments are mosaics made up of multiple SCAs, so this number (1-18) pinpoints which part of the instrument's focal plane collected the observations. It's useful for understanding the instrument's layout and correlating data with specific detector characteristics.
relevance_comment: This identifies the specific detector used, which is commonly useful for understanding instrument context and mosaic data.

description: Frame divisor used on-board when averaging multiple frames into a group
content: This keyword specifies the 'frame divisor' used, which is the number of individual detector frames that were averaged together on-board the spacecraft to form each 'group' of data. This on-board averaging helps reduce read noise but impacts the effective integration time per group and the temporal resolution of the observation. It's crucial for detailed noise modeling and understanding the effective signal-to-noise ratio.
relevance_comment: This parameter is important for understanding the effective integration time, noise properties, and overall data quality, especially for photometry.

description: Data scaling factor ratio between gain=2 and nominal gain=1
content: This keyword specifies a scaling factor applied to the data, representing the ratio of a specific detector gain setting (gain=2) to a nominal gain setting (gain=1). It helps in understanding how detector sensitivity might be adjusted or characterized for different observations.
relevance_comment: This keyword provides instrument-specific calibration information, which is useful for advanced users but less critical for general display or common analysis.

description: Spacecraft clock time adjustment rate (milliseconds/second)
content: This value indicates a very small adjustment rate applied to the spacecraft's internal clock time, measured in milliseconds per second. It is used internally to maintain highly precise timing for observations, correcting for any minor drift in the onboard clock.
relevance_comment: This is a highly technical, internal spacecraft clock calibration parameter with no direct impact on how an end-user views or interprets the astronomical data.

description: Zero frame was read separately (T or F)
content: This boolean keyword indicates whether a 'zero frame' was read out separately from the main exposure. A zero frame is a short exposure taken without light to characterize detector noise and bias, and its separate readout can impact subsequent data processing steps.
relevance_comment: This keyword describes a specific detector readout mode related to calibration, making it relevant for users involved in detailed data processing or quality assessment.

description: Total number of segments in a TSO single exposure

relevance_comment: Proper motion is important for many astrophysical targets, especially for accurate astrometry and target identification over time.

description: Epoch of the proper motion values (UT date string)
comment: Target proper motion epoch in UT
content: This is the specific date (epoch) for which the provided proper motion values (MU_RA, MU_DEC) are valid. It's essential because proper motion describes a rate of change, so this reference point in time is critical for calculating a target's position at any other given date.
relevance_comment: This keyword is critical for correctly interpreting and applying the proper motion values.

description: APT source type for spectroscopy: POINT, EXTENDED, or UNKNOWN
content: This indicates how the observer classified the target source for spectroscopy: as a 'POINT' source (like a star), an 'EXTENDED' source (like a galaxy), or 'UNKNOWN'. This classification guides data reduction and interpretation, as different source types require different analytical approaches.
relevance_comment: This provides critical context for understanding spectroscopic data, especially for source characterization and data reduction choices.

description: Position angle of the aperture used (degrees, east of north)
content: This specifies the orientation of the instrument's aperture or slit on the sky, measured in degrees eastward from North. Understanding this angle is crucial for aligning the observed data with the physical structure of celestial objects, particularly for extended sources with discernible morphology.
relevance_comment: This keyword is essential for understanding the orientation of the instrument's view on the sky, vital for spatial analysis of extended sources.

description: Position angle of the V3 axis (degrees, east of north)
comment: [deg] position angle of V3-axis of JWST
content: This keyword specifies the position angle of the telescope's V3 axis on the sky, measured in degrees eastward from North. The V3 axis serves as a key reference direction for the observatory, helping to define the overall orientation of the spacecraft and its instruments during an observation.
relevance_comment: This is primarily an internal observatory orientation reference, less directly relevant for typical end-user data interpretation than the aperture angle.

description: Right ascension of the telescope V1 axis (degrees)
content: This specifies the Right Ascension coordinate of the telescope's V1 axis. As V1 is the primary pointing axis of the telescope, this value indicates the celestial Right Ascension to which the telescope was pointed at the time of observation.
relevance_comment: This keyword provides the fundamental pointing right ascension of the telescope, which is crucial coordinate information.

description: Declination of the telescope V1 axis (degrees)
content: This keyword specifies the Declination (celestial north-south coordinate) of the telescope's V1 axis, indicating the precise sky location the instrument was pointed towards. It is a fundamental coordinate for pinpointing the target observed and placing the data spatially on the celestial sphere.
relevance_comment: Directly indicates a fundamental celestial coordinate of the observation, essential for locating the data on the sky.

description: Proposal title
content: This keyword provides the official title of the astronomical proposal under which the data were acquired. It offers a concise, plain-language summary of the scientific investigation, giving users immediate context about the observation's purpose and goals.
relevance_comment: Provides immediate scientific context and the purpose of the observed data, highly useful for quick understanding.

description: Principal investigator name
content: This keyword stores the name of the Principal Investigator (PI) who led the astronomical observing program. It identifies the lead scientist or team responsible for the observations, which is useful for attribution, finding related publications, or contacting the original researchers.
relevance_comment: Identifies the lead scientist for the program, useful for attribution and contextual research.

description: Program category assigned by TAC or PI
content: This keyword assigns a broad scientific category to the observing program, typically determined by the Telescope Allocation Committee (TAC) or the PI. It helps classify the data by overarching scientific themes (e.g., Exoplanets, Cosmology), aiding in data discovery and filtering.
relevance_comment: Helps categorize the scientific context of the observation, useful for discovery and filtering data.

description: Program sub-category (FGS, MIRI, NIRCAM, NIRISS, NIRSPEC, SC)
content: For JWST, this keyword specifies a program sub-category that often directly indicates the instrument used for the observation (e.g., MIRI, NIRCAM). Knowing the instrument is critical for understanding data characteristics, capabilities, and for specialized analysis.
relevance_comment: Essential for knowing which specific instrument was used to acquire the data, crucial for understanding data properties.

description: Science category assigned during TAC process
content: This keyword denotes a specific science category assigned during the Telescope Allocation Committee (TAC) review process. It offers a more detailed scientific classification than 'CATEGORY', often relating to the specific target type or astrophysical phenomenon under study.
relevance_comment: Offers a more granular scientific classification, useful for advanced data filtering and understanding research focus.

description: Continuation of previous program number
content: This keyword is an integer identifier that indicates if the current observing program is a continuation of a previous program number. It helps link related observations across different observing cycles or proposals, which is valuable for tracking long-term studies or follow-up campaigns.
relevance_comment: Primarily useful for researchers tracking extended or related observing programs, less for general users.

description: Author of the data or FITS file
content: This keyword specifies the individual or entity responsible for creating or processing the FITS file itself. It provides attribution for the data product, which might be different from the Principal Investigator of the original observations, and is useful for data provenance.
relevance_comment: Provides attribution for the FITS file creator, useful for data provenance rather than direct astronomical content.

description: Number of World Coordinate System axes
comment: number of World Coordinate System axes
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the number of World Coordinate System (WCS) axes defined for the image data. It's a technical detail that tells software how many physical dimensions (e.g., celestial coordinates, spectral channels, time) are mapped by the WCS, enabling proper interpretation of the data array.
relevance_comment: A technical detail about the WCS structure, less directly consumed by end-users than the actual coordinate values.

description: Reference pixel on axis 1 (1-indexed)
comment: Axis 1 pixel coordinate of ref point, 1-indexed
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the pixel coordinate of the reference point along the first image axis (typically the x-axis) within the World Coordinate System. It's a fundamental parameter used in the mathematical conversion from pixel coordinates to real-world coordinates (like Right Ascension on the sky).
relevance_comment: A critical WCS parameter enabling coordinate transformations, but not directly interpreted by typical end-users.

description: Reference pixel on axis 2 (1-indexed)
comment: Axis 2 pixel coordinate of ref point, 1-indexed
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the pixel coordinate on the second axis (y-axis) that corresponds to the sky coordinate given by CRVAL2. It acts as a reference point for the World Coordinate System (WCS) calculations, defining the origin for coordinate transformations.
relevance_comment: It's an internal parameter of the WCS calculation not typically directly needed by end-users.

description: Coordinate value at reference pixel on axis 1 (usually RA in degrees)
comment: Axis 1 coordinate value at reference point
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword provides the celestial coordinate value (usually Right Ascension in degrees) at the reference pixel on the first image axis (x-axis). It defines the absolute sky position of a specific point in the image, typically near its center.
relevance_comment: Essential for knowing the exact sky location an image covers, fundamental for finding objects or cross-referencing with other data.

description: Coordinate value at reference pixel on axis 2 (usually Dec in degrees)
comment: Axis 2 coordinate value at reference point
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword provides the celestial coordinate value (usually Declination in degrees) at the reference pixel on the second image axis (y-axis). Together with CRVAL1, it precisely pins the image to a specific location on the celestial sphere.
relevance_comment: Essential for knowing the exact sky location an image covers, fundamental for finding objects or cross-referencing with other data.

description: Coordinate type for axis 1 (e.g., RA---TAN, GLON-AIT)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword describes the type of celestial coordinate system and the projection used for the first image axis. For example, 'RA---TAN' indicates Right Ascension with a tangent plane projection, crucial for correctly interpreting sky coordinates.
relevance_comment: Very important for understanding what the coordinate values actually represent, ensuring proper interpretation of sky positions.

description: Coordinate type for axis 2 (e.g., DEC--TAN, GLAT-AIT)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Kepler; GALEX; Spitzer; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword describes the type of celestial coordinate system and the projection used for the second image axis. For example, 'DEC--TAN' indicates Declination with a tangent plane projection, complementing CTYPE1 to fully define the sky projection.
relevance_comment: Very important for understanding what the coordinate values actually represent, ensuring proper interpretation of sky positions.

description: Physical unit for axis 1 coordinate (e.g., deg)
content: This keyword specifies the physical unit for the coordinate values on the first axis (e.g., 'deg' for degrees). It is critical for correctly interpreting the numerical values of RA or other sky coordinates.
relevance_comment: Essential for unambiguous interpretation of coordinate values, as a number without units is meaningless in an astronomical context.

description: Physical unit for axis 2 coordinate (e.g., deg)
content: This keyword specifies the physical unit for the coordinate values on the second axis (e.g., 'deg' for degrees). It is critical for correctly interpreting the numerical values of Dec or other sky coordinates.
relevance_comment: Essential for unambiguous interpretation of coordinate values, as a number without units is meaningless in an astronomical context.

description: Coordinate increment along axis 1 (degrees/pixel)
comment: Axis 1 coordinate increment at reference point
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the coordinate increment along the first image axis, typically representing the angular size of a pixel (e.g., degrees per pixel). It describes the spatial resolution or plate scale of the image in one direction.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the image's resolution and field of view, which impacts how much detail is visible.

description: Coordinate increment along axis 2 (degrees/pixel)
comment: Axis 2 coordinate increment at reference point
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the coordinate increment along the second image axis, typically representing the angular size of a pixel (e.g., degrees per pixel). It complements CDELT1 to describe the full spatial resolution of the image.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the image's resolution and field of view, which impacts how much detail is visible.

description: Coordinate transformation matrix element (1,1). Encodes rotation and scale.
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword is an element of the coordinate transformation matrix, which maps pixel coordinates to sky coordinates. It encodes information about the image's scale and any rotation along the first axis. When present with other CD_i_j keywords, it defines a more general WCS transformation than CDELT.
relevance_comment: Primarily an internal component of the WCS calculation; users typically view derived properties like rotation and scale rather than inspecting this matrix element directly.

description: Coordinate transformation matrix element (1,2)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This value is an element of the coordinate description matrix, which defines how pixel coordinates are transformed into world coordinates (like Right Ascension and Declination). It specifically contributes to scaling, rotation, and skew of the image when mapping it to the sky. While not directly viewed by most users, it is crucial for software to accurately display the image with correct celestial coordinates.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

description: Coordinate transformation matrix element (2,1)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This value is an element of the coordinate description matrix, which defines how pixel coordinates are transformed into world coordinates (like Right Ascension and Declination). It specifically contributes to scaling, rotation, and skew of the image when mapping it to the sky. While not directly viewed by most users, it is crucial for software to accurately display the image with correct celestial coordinates.

It specifically contributes to scaling, rotation, and skew of the image when mapping it to the sky. While not directly viewed by most users, it is crucial for software to accurately display the image with correct celestial coordinates.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

description: Linear transformation matrix element (1,1) — alternative to CDi_j
content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

description: Linear transformation matrix element (1,2)
content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

description: Celestial coordinate reference frame (e.g., ICRS, FK5)
comment: Name of the coordinate reference frame
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; TESS; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword specifies the celestial coordinate reference system (e.g., ICRS, FK5) used for the Right Ascension and Declination values in the data. Knowing the reference system is vital for accurately interpreting object positions, comparing data from different sources, and ensuring proper coordinate transformations. For example, positions in ICRS are fixed, while FK5 positions slowly change due to precession.
relevance_comment: This keyword is essential for understanding the specific celestial coordinate system used for the data's RA/Dec values, ensuring correct interpretation and comparisons.

description: Equinox of celestial coordinate system (e.g., 2000.0 for J2000)
missions: FITS Standard; JWST; HST; GALEX; Pan-STARRS
content: This keyword indicates the equinox (epoch) of the celestial coordinate system used for the data's Right Ascension and Declination, most commonly J2000.0. The equinox defines the orientation of the coordinate axes at a specific point in time, which is crucial for precise astrometry. Comparing observations from different eras requires knowing their respective equinoxes to account for precession.
relevance_comment: This keyword specifies the epoch of the celestial coordinate system, crucial for accurate astrometry and for comparing observations across different periods, especially if not J2000.0.

description: Native longitude of the celestial pole (degrees)
comment: Native longitude of the celestial pole
content: This keyword specifies the longitude of the native celestial pole in degrees, which is a technical parameter used in certain World Coordinate System (WCS) projections. It helps define the orientation of the projection, particularly when transforming to or from non-standard coordinate systems or projections. Most end-users will not directly interact with this value, but it is used by WCS software for accurate sky mapping.
relevance_comment: This keyword is a technical detail within the WCS projection definition, primarily used by advanced software to correctly orient complex sky projections.

description: Native latitude of the celestial pole (degrees)
comment: Native latitude of the celestial pole
content: This keyword specifies the native latitude of the celestial pole in degrees, relevant for certain specialized World Coordinate System (WCS) projections. It's a technical parameter defining part of how sky coordinates are mapped onto the image pixels.
relevance_comment: This is a low-level WCS parameter that is not directly consumed by most end-users, who typically work with the transformed RA/Dec coordinates.

description: Spatial footprint of the observation in STC-S (IVOA) format
comment: spatial extent of the observation, footprint
content: This keyword describes the precise spatial footprint of the observation on the sky using the IVOA's STC-S (Space-Time Coordinate System) format. It provides a polygon or region that delineates the exact area covered by the data, useful for database queries and visualization.
relevance_comment: This is highly relevant for understanding the exact sky coverage of an observation, valuable for search, display, and data comparison.

description: Degree of forward SIP polynomial (axis 1)
content: This keyword specifies the degree of the forward 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used for distortion correction along the first image axis (x-axis). SIP coefficients are used to mathematically model and correct for optical distortions in the instrument.
relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model, not directly useful for general users who benefit from the corrected image but don't need the polynomial specifics.

description: Degree of forward SIP polynomial (axis 2)
content: This keyword specifies the degree of the forward 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used for distortion correction along the second image axis (y-axis). SIP coefficients are used to mathematically model and correct for optical distortions in the instrument.
relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model, not directly useful for general users who benefit from the corrected image but don't need the polynomial specifics.

description: Degree of inverse SIP polynomial (axis 1)
content: This keyword specifies the degree of the inverse 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used to reverse distortion corrections along the first image axis. These polynomials allow mapping from distorted image coordinates back to ideal pixel coordinates.
relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model for inverse transformations, not directly useful for general users.

description: Degree of inverse SIP polynomial (axis 2)
content: This keyword specifies the degree of the inverse 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used to reverse distortion corrections along the second image axis. These polynomials allow mapping from distorted image coordinates back to ideal pixel coordinates.
relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model for inverse transformations, not directly useful for general users.

description: Subarray name (FULL if full frame, or named subarray up to 9 chars)
content: This keyword indicates if the observation captured the full detector frame or a specific, smaller region (subarray). It often provides a descriptive name for the subarray used, such as 'FULL' for full frame or a custom name.
relevance_comment: This is useful for understanding whether the image represents the full detector field of view or a specific partial readout, impacting interpretation and visualization.

description: Starting pixel in the full science frame axis 1 direction
content: This keyword indicates the starting pixel column (axis 1) of the observed subarray within the full detector frame. It specifies the leftmost pixel of the subarray in the original detector coordinates.
relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's position, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

description: Starting pixel in the full science frame axis 2 direction
content: This keyword indicates the starting pixel row (axis 2) of the observed subarray within the full detector frame. It specifies the bottommost pixel of the subarray in the original detector coordinates.
relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's position, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

description: Number of pixels in the subarray axis 1 direction
comment: Number of pixels in axis 1 direction
content: This keyword indicates the number of pixels along the first axis (width) of the observed subarray. It defines the size of the captured region in the x-direction.
relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's dimensions, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

description: Number of pixels in the subarray axis 2 direction
comment: Number of pixels in axis 2 direction
content: This keyword specifies the dimension of the detector subarray along its second axis, indicating how many pixels were read out in that direction. It's crucial for understanding the size and extent of the captured image data when only a portion of the full detector was used.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the physical dimensions of a subarray image, relevant for advanced users analyzing instrument modes.

description: Axis and direction of fast detector readout (±1 or ±2)
content: This keyword describes which detector axis (1 or 2) corresponds to the faster readout direction, and whether it's read out in the positive or negative direction. This information is critical for instrument scientists or advanced users performing detailed noise analysis or understanding detector effects.
relevance_comment: Primarily relevant for instrument specialists or those performing detailed detector analysis, not typical end-users.

description: Axis and direction of slow detector readout (±1 or ±2)
content: This keyword indicates which detector axis (1 or 2) corresponds to the slower readout direction, including the positive or negative readout direction. Like FASTAXIS, it's essential for understanding how the detector was operated and for analyzing specific detector artifacts or noise patterns.
relevance_comment: Primarily relevant for instrument specialists or those performing detailed detector analysis, not typical end-users.

description: S&OC PRD aperture name used for telescope pointing
content: This keyword provides the official name of the science aperture used for the observation, as defined by the Spacecraft & Observatory Control (S&OC) system. It tells users exactly which instrument configuration and field of view was selected for the observation, linking the data to specific instrument modes.
relevance_comment: Essential for understanding the specific instrument configuration and field of view used for the observation.

description: Original aperture name as supplied by PPS
content: This keyword stores the initial aperture name as it was specified in the Planning and Scheduling System (PPS) before any internal processing or translation. It can be useful for tracking the origin of an observation's configuration within the mission's planning pipeline.
relevance_comment: Provides context on the aperture name's origin within the planning system, primarily useful for pipeline traceability or specific debugging.

description: Total number of points in the entire dither pattern
comment: Total number of points in dither pattern
content: This keyword specifies the total count of individual positions included in the complete dither pattern for the observation. It helps users understand the overall strategy employed to cover the target area or mitigate detector artifacts through multiple shifted exposures.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the overall observation strategy and the full extent of a dithered observation.

description: Position number within dither pattern
content: This keyword indicates the sequential number of the current observation within its complete dither pattern. It allows users to identify where this specific exposure fits into the larger dither sequence, which is crucial for combining multiple dithered images.
relevance_comment: Crucial for identifying the specific position of an exposure within a dither sequence, vital for data combination.

description: X offset from pattern starting position in SI ideal frame (arcsec)
comment: [arcsec] X offset in SI ideal coordinate frame
content: This keyword provides the offset in arcseconds along the X-axis from the nominal starting point of the dither pattern to the actual observation's position. It's essential for precisely aligning and combining individual dithered images to create a mosaic or improve spatial sampling.
relevance_comment: Essential for accurately aligning and combining individual dithered exposures for improved data quality.

description: Y offset from pattern starting position in SI ideal frame (arcsec)
comment: [arcsec] Y offset in SI ideal coordinate frame
content: This keyword provides the offset in arcseconds along the Y-axis from the nominal starting point of the dither pattern to the current observation's position. Similar to XOFFSET, it's vital for precisely aligning and combining multiple dithered exposures to achieve the intended scientific outcome.
relevance_comment: Essential for accurately aligning and combining individual dithered exposures for improved data quality.

description: Dither pattern type (e.g., FULL, INTRAMODULE, INTRASCA, NONE, CYCLING)
content: This keyword describes the specific strategy or design of the dither pattern employed for the observation. Knowing the pattern type helps users understand the scientific goals behind the dithering (e.g., full coverage, artifact mitigation) and guide their data processing choices.
relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the high-level strategy behind the dither pattern, which informs data analysis and interpretation.

description: Primary dither pattern size (NONE, SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE)
content: This keyword indicates the overall size or extent of the dither pattern used during data acquisition. Dithering is a technique where the telescope is slightly moved between exposures to improve image quality, sample detector pixels better, and remove cosmic rays or detector defects. The size refers to the spatial extent of these shifts.
relevance_comment: Understanding the dither pattern size is useful for advanced users evaluating data quality and spatial resolution.

description: Starting point in the dither cycling pattern (1–311 for MIRI)
content: This specifies which step or position in a multi-point dither sequence was the first one executed for the current observation. When a dither pattern has many points, observations might start at an arbitrary point in the sequence, especially if it's a continuation of a larger program.
relevance_comment: This keyword is primarily an operational detail for pipeline processing and instrument teams, with little direct end-user relevance.

description: Number of points in CYCLING dither pattern (≥3 for MIRI)
comment: Number of points in CYCLING pattern
content: This keyword indicates the total number of distinct positions the telescope visited as part of a cycling dither pattern. A higher number of points generally leads to better sampling of the detector and more robust cosmic ray rejection when the individual dithered images are combined.
relevance_comment: Knowing the number of dither points helps users understand the completeness and quality potential of the combined data, particularly for artifact rejection.

description: Total number of 4-point dither sets (1–10)
comment: Number of 4-point sets in dither set list
content: This specifies how many complete cycles of a smaller, typically 4-point, dither pattern were executed. Some dither strategies involve repeating a foundational pattern multiple times to achieve specific noise reduction or image quality goals.
relevance_comment: This provides insight into the observation's redundancy and helps assess the potential for noise reduction or coverage for specific dither strategies.

description: Starting set number of the 4-point dither sets (1–10)
comment: Starting 4-point set in dither set list
content: This keyword indicates which specific set number of a repeated dither pattern was the first one observed within a larger sequence. It tracks the starting point for multi-set dither strategies, similar to tracking the starting point within a single dither pattern.
relevance_comment: This is an internal detail for data pipeline and instrument teams, holding little direct value for typical end-user analysis.

description: Dither direction: POSITIVE or NEGATIVE
content: This indicates the general direction of the dither steps, often referring to whether the movements are sequentially positive or negative in a given coordinate axis. This can be relevant for understanding the precise sequence of small movements performed by the telescope.
relevance_comment: This is a highly specific operational detail of dither execution that rarely impacts the final scientific interpretation for most end-users.

description: Dither optimization type: POINT-SOURCE or EXTENDED-SOURCE
content: This keyword describes whether the dither pattern was specifically designed to optimize observations of point sources (like stars) or extended sources (like galaxies). Different optimization types use dither step sizes and patterns to best sample the target morphology and mitigate detector effects.
relevance_comment: This is very useful as it directly tells the user if the dither strategy was appropriate for the observed target type, influencing photometry or surface brightness measurements.

description: Sparse cycling dither positions list
content: This keyword provides a list or description of the specific positions visited during a sparse cycling dither pattern. Sparse dithers typically involve larger steps designed to move a target across different detector regions to mitigate artifacts or improve flat-fielding.
relevance_comment: For advanced users, knowing the exact dither positions can be crucial for understanding detector coverage, artifact mitigation, and for re-processing data.

description: MIRI MRS channel for which dither is optimized
content: This keyword specifies which of the MIRI Medium Resolution Spectrometer (MRS) channels the dither pattern was specifically optimized for. The MRS has multiple channels covering different wavelength ranges with varying pixel scales, requiring channel-specific dither strategies.
relevance_comment: This is important for MIRI MRS users to confirm the dither strategy was appropriate for the analyzed channel, impacting spectral extraction and data quality.

description: NIRSpec MSA nod pattern type (NONE, 2-, 3-, or 5-SHUTTER-SLITLET)
content: For NIRSpec Multi-Object Spectrometer (MOS) observations, this keyword describes the type of 'nodding' pattern used. Nodding is a technique where the telescope moves the target between different micro-shutter array (MSA) shutters to allow for accurate background subtraction and improved data quality, especially for faint targets.
relevance_comment: This is crucial for NIRSpec MOS users to understand how background subtraction was handled, which directly impacts the quality of extracted spectra.

description: NIRCam primary dither specification with number of points
content: This keyword indicates the specific type of primary dither pattern used during the observation, along with the total number of points in that pattern. Dithering involves slightly shifting the telescope's pointing between exposures to improve image quality, fill in detector gaps, and mitigate cosmic ray effects.
relevance_comment: This keyword is useful for advanced users to understand the observation strategy and assess data quality or completeness.

description: Number of points in FGS primary dither pattern
comment: Number of points in primary dither pattern
content: This keyword specifies the total number of individual pointing positions in the primary dither pattern performed by the Fine Guidance Sensor (FGS) during the observation. A higher number of dither points generally allows for better sampling and artifact rejection.
relevance_comment: Understanding the number of dither points helps advanced users assess the quality and coverage of combined data products.

description: Subpixel dither pattern type (STANDARD, SMALL-GRID-DITHER, NONE)
content: This keyword describes the type of subpixel dither pattern applied, which is designed to sample the sky at resolutions finer than a single detector pixel. Common types include 'STANDARD' or 'SMALL-GRID-DITHER'. Using a subpixel dither improves the effective resolution of the final image.
relevance_comment: This is valuable for users interested in high-resolution imaging, as it directly impacts the achievable detail in the final processed data.

description: Number of points in subpixel dither pattern
comment: Number of points in subpixel dither pattern
content: This keyword indicates the number of distinct pointing positions in the subpixel dither pattern. More points in a subpixel dither pattern contribute to a more finely sampled final image, which is crucial for achieving optimal spatial resolution.
relevance_comment: Knowing the number of subpixel dither points is useful for advanced users evaluating the potential resolution and quality of the final image.

description: NIRCam small grid dither pattern name
content: This keyword specifies the particular name or configuration of the NIRCam small grid dither pattern used. Small grid dithers are a specialized type of dither often employed for specific science cases requiring precise subpixel sampling or mosaic patterns.
relevance_comment: This keyword is relevant for specialized NIRCam users who need to know the exact dither strategy for their specific analysis.

description: NIRISS direct image dither number of points
comment: Number of points in direct image dither pattern
content: This keyword indicates the number of dither points specifically used for direct imaging observations with the NIRISS instrument. This value informs how many distinct positions the instrument observed as part of its dither sequence.
relevance_comment: This is useful for NIRISS instrument specialists or advanced users analyzing direct imaging data to understand data coverage.

description: MIRI LRS number of steps in the spatial direction
content: This keyword specifies the total number of steps or positions taken in the spatial direction during a MIRI Low-Resolution Spectroscopy (LRS) observation. These steps are part of a dither pattern to improve spatial sampling or remove detector artifacts.
relevance_comment: This keyword is valuable for users working with MIRI LRS data to understand the spatial sampling of their spectra.

description: MIRI LRS distance between steps in the spatial direction (arcsec)
content: This keyword provides the physical distance, in arcseconds, between successive steps in the spatial direction for a MIRI LRS observation. This value quantifies the actual offset applied during spatial dithering.
relevance_comment: This precise measurement is highly useful for advanced users performing detailed spatial analysis or source extraction from MIRI LRS data.

description: MIRI LRS position number within spatial steps
content: This keyword indicates the specific position number of the current exposure within the larger sequence of spatial steps for a MIRI LRS observation. It helps to identify where this particular frame fits into the overall dither pattern.
relevance_comment: This keyword is important for advanced users needing to track and combine individual frames from a dither sequence.

description: MIRI LRS number of steps in the spectral direction
content: This keyword specifies the total number of steps or positions taken in the spectral direction during a MIRI Low-Resolution Spectroscopy (LRS) observation. These steps are part of a dither pattern designed to mitigate fringing and improve spectral fidelity.
relevance_comment: This keyword is valuable for users working with MIRI LRS data to understand the spectral sampling and artifact mitigation strategy.

description: MIRI LRS distance between steps in the spectral direction (arcsec)
content: This keyword indicates the physical distance in arcseconds between successive exposures when dithered along the spectral direction for MIRI LRS observations. It's important for understanding how the instrument sampled the spectrum to mitigate detector artifacts or improve resolution.
relevance_comment: This details a specific dither parameter, useful for understanding data quality and processing, but not for casual viewing.

description: MIRI LRS position number within spectral steps
content: This identifies the specific position within a sequence of spectral dither steps for MIRI LRS observations. It helps researchers track which part of the dither pattern a particular exposure corresponds to, crucial for co-adding and artifact removal.
relevance_comment: This indicates the dither position, useful for advanced data processing and quality control.

description: MIRI LRS spatial offset from pattern starting position (arcsec)
content: This keyword specifies the spatial offset in arcseconds from the starting point of the dither pattern for MIRI LRS observations. It helps determine the precise on-sky position of the observation relative to the overall pattern.
relevance_comment: This describes a spatial offset within a dither pattern, relevant for precise astrometry and advanced data reduction.
